  • Tutorial: Realistic Heating Controllers

Tutorial: Realistic Heating Controllers

Why perfect tracking is unrealistic

The ideal PI controller in GreenhousesJL tracks the setpoint exactly (T_air = 16.00°C). In reality:

ImperfectionReal-world valueEnergy impactSource
PI tracking error (RMSE)1.0–2.5°C+8–20% overconsumptionRodriguez et al. (2015)
Dead band (standard)±1°C−5–10% energyBakker (1995)
Boiler cycling losses−5–15% seasonal η+5–15% fuelASHRAE (2020)
Real HP COP vs rated60–75% of rated+25–40% electricityStaffell et al. (2012)
Optimal control vs fixed SP—−10–25% energyvan Henten (1994)
MPC vs PI—−15–25% energyBlasco et al. (2007)

Key insight: Perfect tracking is NOT the most energy-efficient strategy (van Henten 1994, Seginer 1993). Temperature integration (allowing ±2–4°C within 24h) saves 10–20% with no yield loss for tomato (Korner & van Straten 2008).

Controller modes

IdealPI (default for validation)

p = GreenhouseParams(tmy; ..., controller=IdealPI())

Perfect continuous tracking. Used for cross-validation against EnergyPlus and OpenModelica.

RealisticPI (for energy estimation)

p = GreenhouseParams(tmy; ..., controller=StandardController())
PresetDead bandPart-load factorUse case
StandardController()±1.0°C0.85Standard Dutch greenhouse
AggressiveSavingPI()±2.0°C0.80Energy-priority operation
TightControlPI()±0.5°C0.92Sensitive crops

Custom configuration:

ctrl = RealisticPI(dead_band=1.5, part_load_factor=0.88)

Dead band physics

The dead band creates a hysteresis loop around the setpoint:

Heating ON:  when T_air < T_sp - dead_band  (too cold)
Heating OFF: when T_air > T_sp              (setpoint reached)

With a ±1°C dead band and setpoint 16°C:

  • Heater turns ON at T_air = 15°C
  • Heater turns OFF at T_air = 16°C
  • Average temperature ≈ 15.5°C (0.5°C below setpoint)

This reduces energy because the greenhouse spends time below the setpoint, reducing heat losses through the cover. Bailey (1988) quantifies: each 1°C decrease in average temperature saves 6–8% of heating energy.

Part-load degradation

Real heating equipment is less efficient at partial load (Staffell et al. 2012):

\[\eta_{real} = \eta_{rated} \times PLF\]

where PLF (part-load factor) accounts for:

  • Boiler cycling: short on/off cycles waste heat in the flue (ASHRAE: 5–15% loss)
  • HP defrost cycles: air-source HP must periodically defrost (5–12% COP reduction)
  • Standby losses: equipment keeps warm even when not heating
EquipmentTypical PLFSource
Gas boiler0.85–0.95ASHRAE (2020)
Air-source HP0.70–0.85Staffell et al. (2012)
Ground-source HP0.85–0.92—
CHP0.80–0.90—

Simulation comparison

14,000 m² greenhouse, Brussels December, gas boiler + overhead pipes:

ControllerT_meanEnergyΔE vs ideal
Ideal PI16.00°C7.29 kWh/m²ref
Tight ±0.5°C15.95°C7.21 kWh/m²−1%
Standard ±1°C15.72°C7.01 kWh/m²−4%
Savings ±2°C15.18°C6.55 kWh/m²−10%

The −10% for the savings controller is consistent with Bakker (1995): ±2°C dead band saves 5–10%.

Combined effects: controller + heating system + crop

The total energy gap between simulation (ideal PI) and reality is the product of all imperfections:

\[E_{real} = E_{ideal} \times (1 - \Delta_{deadband}) \times \frac{1}{PLF} \times (1 + \Delta_{infiltration})\]

Example for a gas boiler greenhouse:

  • Ideal PI: 7.29 kWh/m²
  • Dead band ±1°C: −4% → 7.0 kWh/m²
  • Part-load factor 0.85: ÷0.85 → 8.2 kWh/m² fuel consumed
  • Real infiltration (+15%): × 1.15 → 9.5 kWh/m² fuel consumed

The simulation-to-reality gap is typically +10–30% (Katzin et al. 2022), which matches this breakdown.

References

  • Bakker, J.C. (1995). Greenhouse climate control: constraints and limitations. Acta Hort., 399.
  • Bailey, B.J. (1988). Principles of environmental control. In: Greenhouse Technology and Management.
  • Tap, R.F. (2000). Economics-based optimal control of greenhouse tomato crop production. PhD, Wageningen.
  • van Henten, E.J. (1994). Greenhouse climate management: an optimal control approach. PhD, Wageningen.
  • Rodriguez, F. et al. (2015). Modeling and Control of Greenhouse Crop Growth. Springer.
  • Staffell, I. et al. (2012). A review of domestic heat pumps. Energy Environ. Sci., 5(11).
  • Blasco, X. et al. (2007). Model-based predictive control of greenhouse climate. Comp. Elec. Agric., 55(1).
  • Korner, O. & van Straten, G. (2008). Decision support for dynamic greenhouse climate control. Comp. Elec. Agric., 60(1).
  • Katzin, D. et al. (2022). Process-based greenhouse climate models. Agricultural Systems, 198.
